clause_types.name: AI-Generated Output Ownership
clause_types.description: Allocates intellectual property rights in outputs generated by artificial intelligence systems, including text, code, images, data analyses, and other machine-generated content. Addresses the fundamental legal uncertainty that AI-generated works may not qualify for copyright protection in many jurisdictions because they lack a human author, and establishes contractual ownership irrespective of statutory copyright status.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define 'AI Output' precisely to distinguish from human-authored deliverables. Specify whether ownership vests in the party providing the prompt, the party operating the AI system, or the party commissioning the output. Address the copyright status uncertainty by including contractual assignment of any rights that may exist, combined with a licence fallback if copyright does not subsist. Consider whether the AI output incorporates or is derived from the provider's training data or model weights and address contamination risk. Include representations regarding originality and non-infringement. Address moral rights where applicable. Specify whether the customer may claim authorship. Include a schedule defining categories of AI output and their ownership treatment.

variations.customer-owns: All AI-generated outputs vest in the customer upon creation and payment
variations.provider-owns-licence-granted: Provider retains ownership of AI outputs but grants customer a broad licence
variations.shared-ownership: Joint ownership with each party having independent exploitation rights in defined fields

clause_types.name: AI Training Data Rights and Licensing
clause_types.description: Governs the rights to use data for training, fine-tuning, validating, and evaluating AI and machine learning models. Addresses whether the data provider grants a licence for training purposes, whether the AI provider may use customer data to improve its general models, and the restrictions on data usage including opt-out rights. Critical given the evolving legal landscape around fair use, text and data mining exceptions, and the EU AI Act transparency obligations.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define 'Training Data' to include all data used for model training, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, evaluation, and validation. Specify the licence scope: whether data may be used only for the customer's dedicated model or also for the provider's general foundation model. Address opt-out rights explicitly per EU DSM Directive Art 4 text and data mining exceptions. Include restrictions on combining training data with third-party data. Require the provider to maintain data provenance records. Address data retention after model training (whether raw data must be deleted after training or may be retained). Specify audit rights over training data usage. Address the risk of memorisation and regurgitation of training data in model outputs. Include compliance with copyright and database rights in all relevant jurisdictions.

variations.restricted: Data may be used solely for the customer's dedicated model; no use in general model training
variations.opt-out: Data used for general model improvement unless customer exercises opt-out right
variations.broad-licence: Broad licence for all AI training purposes with anonymisation requirements

clause_types.name: AI Model Governance Framework
clause_types.description: Establishes the governance structure for managing AI models throughout their lifecycle, including development, testing, deployment, monitoring, and decommissioning. Defines roles and responsibilities for model risk management, change control, performance monitoring, and incident response. Implements the organisational measures required under the EU AI Act for high-risk AI systems and aligns with emerging responsible AI frameworks.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the governance body and its decision-making authority (AI ethics board, model risk committee). Specify model risk management requirements: model inventory, risk classification aligned with EU AI Act risk categories (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal), model validation and testing standards, ongoing performance monitoring, and drift detection. Address change control: who may approve model updates, retraining triggers, rollback procedures. Include incident escalation paths for model failures or harmful outputs. Require documentation: model cards, data sheets, impact assessments. Specify reporting cadence and metrics. Address regulatory engagement and notification obligations. Define roles: model owner, model validator (independent), AI ethics officer, data steward.

variations.light-touch: Governance proportionate to low-risk AI applications with annual review cycle
variations.enterprise: Full governance framework with independent model validation and quarterly review
variations.regulated: Governance meeting regulatory requirements for high-risk AI systems under EU AI Act

clause_types.name: AI Explainability and Transparency Requirements
clause_types.description: Imposes obligations on the AI provider to ensure that AI system decisions and outputs can be explained to affected individuals, regulators, and internal stakeholders. Addresses the technical explainability of model behaviour, the provision of meaningful information about the logic involved, and the transparency obligations under the EU AI Act and GDPR Art 13-14. Essential for high-risk AI systems and automated decision-making affecting individuals' rights.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the level of explainability required: global explanations (how the model works generally), local explanations (why a specific decision was made), or both. Specify the audience for explanations (data subjects, regulators, internal audit) and the appropriate level of technical detail for each. Require documentation of model architecture, training methodology, key features, and known limitations. Address GDPR Art 13(2)(f) and Art 14(2)(g) obligations to provide meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decision-making. Specify response times for explanation requests. Address trade secret tensions: the provider may resist full model transparency citing IP protection. Include tiered disclosure: full technical details under NDA to the customer's audit team, simplified explanations for data subjects. Require impact assessments documenting potential effects on individuals.

variations.basic: High-level model documentation and simplified explanations for end users
variations.detailed: Full technical explainability including feature importance, decision paths, and confidence scores
variations.regulatory: Explainability meeting EU AI Act Art 13 requirements for high-risk systems with audit trail

clause_types.name: AI Bias Monitoring and Fairness Obligations
clause_types.description: Requires ongoing monitoring and mitigation of bias in AI systems to ensure fair and non-discriminatory outcomes. Addresses algorithmic bias across protected characteristics, establishes testing methodologies, defines acceptable fairness metrics, and creates remediation obligations when bias is detected. Implements the non-discrimination requirements of the EU AI Act for high-risk systems and aligns with equality legislation.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the protected characteristics to be monitored (race, gender, age, disability, etc.) aligned with applicable equality legislation. Specify fairness metrics: demographic parity, equalised odds, predictive parity, or calibration. Require pre-deployment bias testing with documented results. Mandate ongoing bias monitoring at defined intervals (monthly or quarterly). Establish bias thresholds and triggers for remediation. Define remediation obligations: timeline for correction, interim measures (suspend automated decisions, revert to human review), root cause analysis. Require a bias incident register. Address intersectional bias (combinations of protected characteristics). Include reporting obligations to the customer and, where applicable, to regulators. Specify testing datasets and methodology transparency. Address the tension between bias mitigation and model accuracy.

variations.standard: Quarterly bias assessment with reporting and remediation within 60 days
variations.enhanced: Monthly monitoring with real-time bias alerts and remediation within 30 days
variations.regulatory: Bias monitoring meeting EU AI Act Art 9 and Art 10 requirements for high-risk systems

clause_types.name: AI-Specific Liability Allocation
clause_types.description: Defines the allocation of liability for harms caused by AI system outputs, errors, or failures between the AI provider, the deployer, and the end user. Addresses the unique liability challenges of AI systems including unpredictable outputs, hallucinations, emergent behaviours, and the difficulty of establishing causation in complex model chains. Must account for the EU AI Liability Directive proposals and existing product liability frameworks.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Establish clear liability boundaries between the AI provider (model accuracy, training data quality, system reliability) and the deployer/customer (appropriate use, human oversight, input data quality). Address liability for AI hallucinations and factually incorrect outputs. Specify whether the AI provider warrants output accuracy and to what standard. Define the liability regime: fault-based, strict liability for high-risk systems, or shared liability with defined proportions. Address the burden of proof for AI-related claims (consider the EU AI Liability Directive's proposed rebuttable presumption of causation). Include specific liability carve-outs for misuse, use outside intended purpose, or failure to implement required human oversight. Address downstream liability to third parties affected by AI decisions. Specify whether consequential damages exclusions apply to AI-specific harms.

variations.provider-liable: Provider assumes primary liability for AI output quality with defined exceptions
variations.shared-liability: Liability split by function: provider for model, customer for deployment and oversight
variations.customer-assumes-risk: Customer assumes risk of AI outputs subject to provider meeting defined accuracy thresholds

clause_types.name: Algorithmic Audit Rights
clause_types.description: Grants the customer or an independent third party the right to audit the AI system's algorithms, training data, and decision-making processes. Addresses the growing regulatory expectation for algorithmic accountability and the practical challenges of auditing complex AI systems, including the tension between transparency and trade secret protection.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the scope of audit: model architecture, training data provenance, performance metrics, bias testing results, decision logs, and compliance documentation. Specify who may conduct the audit: customer's internal team, independent third-party auditor, or regulator. Address frequency: annual scheduled audits with additional audits on cause (material performance degradation, bias incident, regulatory investigation). Require the provider to maintain auditable records per EU AI Act Art 12 logging requirements. Address trade secret protection: audit conducted under NDA, clean room procedures for sensitive IP, auditor qualification requirements. Specify the provider's cooperation obligations: access to personnel, documentation, test environments. Define audit deliverables: findings report, remediation recommendations, compliance attestation. Address cost allocation. Include remediation obligations with timelines for findings.

variations.full-access: Direct access to model code, training data, and infrastructure for audit
variations.report-based: Provider commissions independent audit and shares report with customer
variations.hybrid: Annual report-based audit with on-cause right to conduct direct audit

clause_types.name: Synthetic Data Generation and Usage Rights
clause_types.description: Governs the creation, ownership, and permitted uses of synthetic data generated by AI systems, including privacy-preserving synthetic datasets derived from real data. Addresses whether synthetic data retains the characteristics and restrictions of the source data, the quality assurance requirements for synthetic data, and the regulatory treatment of synthetic data under data protection laws.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define 'Synthetic Data' precisely: algorithmically generated data that mimics the statistical properties of real data without containing actual personal data or confidential information. Specify ownership of synthetic data outputs. Address whether synthetic data derived from personal data constitutes personal data under GDPR (depends on re-identification risk). Require privacy impact assessments before synthetic data generation from personal data. Specify quality metrics for synthetic data: statistical fidelity, utility preservation, privacy guarantees (differential privacy parameters). Address the permitted uses of synthetic data: model training, testing, sharing with third parties. Include restrictions on reverse-engineering source data from synthetic outputs. Require documentation of the generation methodology. Specify retention and deletion obligations for both source and synthetic data.

variations.privacy-first: Synthetic data must meet formal differential privacy guarantees before any use
variations.utility-first: Focus on statistical fidelity with privacy as secondary constraint
variations.restricted: Synthetic data subject to same restrictions as source data

clause_types.name: Prompt Engineering Intellectual Property
clause_types.description: Addresses the ownership and protection of prompts, prompt libraries, prompt chains, and prompt engineering methodologies used to elicit specific outputs from AI systems. As prompt engineering becomes a material commercial asset, contracts must address whether prompts constitute protectable IP, who owns them, and what restrictions apply to their use, disclosure, and reverse-engineering.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define 'Prompts' broadly to include system prompts, user prompts, prompt templates, prompt chains, few-shot examples, and meta-prompts. Address the uncertain copyright status of prompts (may be too short or too functional for copyright protection in many jurisdictions). Establish contractual protection irrespective of copyright status through confidentiality and restrictive covenants. Specify ownership: customer owns custom prompts developed for its use case; provider retains ownership of its base prompt library. Address reverse-engineering restrictions: prohibit extraction, reconstruction, or reverse-engineering of the provider's system prompts. Include non-compete or non-use restrictions for prompt methodologies. Address whether prompt optimisation insights derived from customer usage revert to the provider. Specify prompt versioning and documentation requirements.

variations.customer-owns-all: All prompts developed during engagement vest in the customer
variations.split-ownership: Customer owns custom prompts; provider retains base library and generic optimisation insights
variations.provider-owns-licence-granted: Provider owns all prompts and grants customer a use licence

clause_types.name: Foundation Model Licence Terms
clause_types.description: Governs the licensing of large foundation models (LLMs, vision models, multimodal models) including the scope of permitted use, modification rights, redistribution restrictions, and compliance with the EU AI Act obligations for general-purpose AI models. Addresses the unique characteristics of foundation models: their general-purpose nature, the distinction between model weights and model outputs, and the layered licensing common in the AI ecosystem.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the licensed model precisely: model name, version, model weights, associated documentation, and any fine-tuning data included. Specify the licence type: proprietary API access, open-weight licence (e.g., Llama, Mistral), or fully open-source. Address permitted uses: commercial vs research, internal vs customer-facing, geographic restrictions. Specify modification rights: fine-tuning, distillation, quantisation, and whether derivative models are subject to the same licence. Address redistribution: may the licensee redistribute model weights, host the model for third parties, or create derivative services. Include EU AI Act GPAI model obligations: technical documentation, copyright policy, training data summary. Address acceptable use policies and prohibited uses (generation of CSAM, weapons, disinformation). Specify benchmarking and evaluation rights. Include model update and support obligations.

variations.proprietary-api: Access via API only; no access to model weights; provider controls all infrastructure
variations.open-weight: Model weights provided under permissive licence with use restrictions
variations.enterprise: Full model weights with fine-tuning rights, on-premises deployment, and dedicated support

clause_types.name: AI SaaS Performance Standards
clause_types.description: Defines the performance metrics and quality standards specific to AI-powered SaaS platforms, going beyond traditional uptime SLAs to address model accuracy, inference latency, throughput, and output quality. Recognises that for AI services, availability alone is insufficient and that the quality and reliability of AI outputs are the core service attributes.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define AI-specific performance metrics: model accuracy (precision, recall, F1 score), inference latency (p50, p95, p99), throughput (requests per second), output quality scores, hallucination rate. Establish baseline performance levels measured during acceptance testing or a defined benchmark period. Specify measurement methodology: who measures, what test datasets are used, how edge cases are handled. Address model degradation: define acceptable performance variance from baseline and triggers for remediation. Include service credits specific to AI performance failures (distinct from availability credits). Address the challenge of measuring output quality for generative AI (human evaluation, automated quality scoring, customer satisfaction metrics). Specify reporting obligations: performance dashboards, monthly quality reports. Include benchmarking rights against competing services. Address seasonal or usage-pattern variations in performance.

variations.accuracy-focused: Primary metrics are accuracy, precision, and recall with defined minimum thresholds
variations.latency-focused: Primary metrics are inference latency and throughput for real-time applications
variations.comprehensive: Combined accuracy, latency, and quality metrics with tiered service credits

clause_types.name: AI API Service Level Agreement
clause_types.description: Establishes the service level commitments for AI APIs including availability, rate limits, latency guarantees, error rates, and the remedies for failure to meet agreed levels. Addresses AI-specific SLA considerations such as model version consistency, token throughput, context window availability, and the impact of provider-side model updates on API behaviour.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define API availability target (e.g., 99.9% monthly uptime). Specify measurement methodology and exclusions. Define rate limits and burst capacity. Establish latency targets: time to first token, total generation time, and p95/p99 latency bounds. Specify error rate limits (5xx responses, timeout rates). Address model version pinning: whether the customer can lock a specific model version and for how long. Define token throughput guarantees (tokens per minute/hour). Address context window availability and any queuing mechanisms. Specify service credit structure for SLA breaches. Include API deprecation notice requirements (minimum 6 months). Address backwards compatibility: define what constitutes a breaking change. Specify support SLAs for API issues: response times by severity. Include fair use limitations and overage pricing.

variations.standard: 99.9% availability with standard rate limits and best-effort latency
variations.premium: 99.95% availability with dedicated capacity, guaranteed latency, and priority support
variations.enterprise: 99.99% availability with reserved capacity, model version pinning, and dedicated endpoints

clause_types.name: AI Data Portability and Model Portability
clause_types.description: Ensures the customer's ability to extract and migrate its data, fine-tuned models, training configurations, and operational insights from the AI provider's platform. Goes beyond traditional data portability to address AI-specific assets including model weights, embeddings, prompt libraries, and evaluation datasets that represent significant customer investment and lock-in risk.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define all portable assets: raw data, processed/enriched data, fine-tuned model weights, embeddings, prompt libraries, evaluation datasets, training configurations, hyperparameters, performance benchmarks, and operational logs. Specify export formats: standard data formats (CSV, JSON, Parquet), model formats (ONNX, SafeTensors, GGUF), and embedding formats. Establish export timelines: data available within 30 days, model weights within 60 days. Address the portability of fine-tuned models built on the provider's foundation model (may be restricted by the foundation model licence). Specify whether the provider must assist with migration to a competitor platform. Include ongoing portability testing rights (annual test exports). Address portability of integrated workflows and API configurations. Specify cost allocation for export activities. Address data integrity verification post-export.

variations.data-only: Export of customer data in standard formats; models remain with provider
variations.full-portability: Export of all customer data, fine-tuned models, prompts, and configurations
variations.assisted-migration: Full portability plus provider-assisted migration to replacement platform

clause_types.name: AI Vendor Lock-In Prevention
clause_types.description: Contractual protections designed to prevent or mitigate the risk of becoming commercially or technically dependent on a single AI vendor. Addresses the specific lock-in vectors in AI services: proprietary data formats, non-portable fine-tuned models, API-specific prompt engineering, accumulated training data, and the switching costs associated with AI platform migration.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Identify and address each lock-in vector: data format lock-in (require open formats), model lock-in (portability of fine-tuned weights), prompt lock-in (ownership and portability of prompts), integration lock-in (standard APIs), knowledge lock-in (documentation of configurations and methodologies). Require the provider to use open standards where available (ONNX for models, OpenAPI for APIs). Include interoperability commitments. Specify regular export testing rights. Prohibit the provider from taking actions that increase lock-in (proprietary format changes without migration tools). Require multi-cloud deployment options or architecture portability. Include competitive benchmarking rights. Address pricing lock-in: cap on price increases, most-favoured-customer pricing. Specify exit assistance obligations and transition support. Include anti-lock-in representations from the provider.

variations.open-standards: Provider must use open standards and formats throughout
variations.exit-focused: Focus on exit rights and transition assistance rather than preventing lock-in during term
variations.multi-vendor: Architecture designed for multi-vendor deployment from inception

clause_types.name: AI Technology Escrow Arrangement
clause_types.description: Provides for the deposit of AI model weights, training data, source code, and associated documentation with an independent escrow agent, with release to the customer upon defined trigger events. Adapts traditional software escrow concepts for AI systems, addressing the unique challenges of escrowing large model files, training pipelines, and the infrastructure knowledge needed to operationalise a released model.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the escrow materials comprehensively: model weights (all versions), training code, inference code, training data or data provenance records, configuration files, deployment scripts, model documentation, and operational runbooks. Specify the escrow agent and deposit schedule (initial deposit plus updates with each material version change). Define release triggers: provider insolvency, material breach, cessation of business, failure to maintain the service, regulatory action preventing service. Address verification: require regular testing that escrowed materials can reproduce a functioning AI system. Address the practical challenge of model size (models may be hundreds of gigabytes). Specify the customer's rights upon release: perpetual licence to use, modify, and deploy. Address the dependency chain: escrow must include third-party library requirements and infrastructure specifications. Include cost allocation for escrow maintenance and verification.

variations.full-escrow: Complete model weights, training data, and all supporting materials deposited
variations.code-escrow: Source code and training pipeline escrowed; model weights available via separate mechanism
variations.verification-tested: Full escrow with annual build-and-deploy verification by independent party

clause_types.name: AI Cloud Exit Planning and Transition
clause_types.description: Establishes the contractual framework for an orderly exit from an AI cloud services provider, including transition timelines, data migration, service continuity during transition, and the provider's cooperation obligations. Addresses the heightened exit complexity of AI services where the customer's operational dependency extends beyond data to encompass trained models, accumulated learning, and integrated AI workflows.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the exit triggers: termination for any reason, expiry without renewal, change of control, material service degradation. Specify the transition period: minimum 6-12 months during which the provider must continue service at current levels and pricing. Define transition assistance obligations: data export, model migration support, knowledge transfer, API mapping to replacement provider. Address continued access to the AI service during transition. Specify the exit plan development obligation: require a documented exit plan within 90 days of contract execution, updated annually. Define the provider's staffing obligations during transition. Address data and model deletion obligations post-transition (with certification). Include pricing for transition services: at-cost or pre-agreed rates, not at premium pricing. Specify dispute resolution for exit-related disagreements. Address the handover of operational documentation and institutional knowledge.

variations.standard: 6-month transition period with defined assistance obligations
variations.extended: 12-month transition with dedicated transition team and phased migration
variations.pre-planned: Exit plan documented at contract inception with annual testing and validation

clause_types.name: AI Multi-Tenancy Isolation Requirements
clause_types.description: Defines the isolation requirements for AI services operating in a multi-tenant architecture, where multiple customers share the same AI infrastructure, models, or processing pipelines. Addresses the unique risks of multi-tenancy in AI contexts including model contamination between tenants, data leakage through model memorisation, prompt injection attacks, and the potential for one tenant's usage patterns to degrade another's service quality.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the isolation model: shared infrastructure with logical isolation, dedicated model instances, or fully isolated compute. Specify data isolation: ensure no tenant's data is accessible to another tenant or used in another tenant's model training. Address model isolation: whether the model processes multiple tenants' requests simultaneously and the risk of cross-tenant data leakage through model context. Require technical controls: tenant-specific encryption keys, network segmentation, access controls, audit logging per tenant. Address the noisy neighbour problem: one tenant's high usage degrading another's performance (require resource guarantees or fair-use limits). Specify security testing requirements: penetration testing of isolation boundaries, prompt injection testing. Address compliance: some regulated industries require dedicated instances (financial services under DORA, healthcare under HIPAA). Include the provider's representations regarding isolation effectiveness. Define incident response for isolation breaches.

variations.logical-isolation: Shared infrastructure with software-enforced isolation and encryption
variations.dedicated-model: Dedicated model instance per tenant on shared infrastructure
variations.full-isolation: Dedicated compute, storage, and model instance per tenant

clause_types.name: AI Data Residency and Processing Location
clause_types.description: Specifies the geographic locations where AI training, inference, and data storage may occur, addressing the regulatory requirements for data localisation and the practical challenges of constraining AI processing to specific regions. Particularly relevant for AI services where training may occur in one jurisdiction, inference in another, and data storage in a third, creating complex cross-border data flow scenarios.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the permitted processing locations for each activity: data storage, model training, model inference, and data analysis. Distinguish between data-at-rest and data-in-transit location requirements. Address the GDPR transfer restrictions for personal data processed by AI systems, including the adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses, and Binding Corporate Rules requirements. Specify whether the provider may use global infrastructure for non-personal data components (model weights, anonymised data). Address the use of GPU cloud providers: where are GPUs located, and does data leave the contracted jurisdiction during processing. Require notification before any change in processing location. Include the right to restrict processing to specific regions. Address edge computing and on-device AI processing. Specify audit rights over processing locations. Address sovereign cloud requirements for government-related workloads.

variations.eu-only: All processing, training, and storage confined to EU/EEA locations
variations.regional-flexible: Primary region specified with permitted failover regions
variations.data-classified: Different residency requirements based on data classification level

clause_types.name: AI Cloud Infrastructure Audit Rights
clause_types.description: Grants the customer the right to audit the AI provider's cloud infrastructure, security controls, and operational practices. Extends traditional IT audit rights to address AI-specific concerns including GPU cluster security, model serving infrastructure, training pipeline integrity, and the security of the inference environment where customer data is processed.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define audit scope to include: physical and logical security of AI infrastructure, GPU cluster configuration, model serving environment security, training pipeline controls, data handling procedures, access management, incident response capabilities, and business continuity arrangements. Specify audit types: documentary review, on-site inspection, penetration testing, compliance assessment. Address the multi-tenant challenge: how to audit shared infrastructure without compromising other tenants. Require the provider to maintain industry certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, ISO 42001). Specify audit frequency: annual scheduled audit with additional audits on cause. Address cost allocation. Define remediation obligations and timelines for audit findings. Include the right to audit sub-processors and GPU cloud providers. Specify auditor qualification requirements and confidentiality obligations.

variations.certification-based: Provider maintains SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001; shares reports in lieu of direct audit
variations.direct-access: Customer or third-party auditor conducts direct infrastructure audit
variations.hybrid: Annual certification review with on-cause direct audit rights

clause_types.name: AI System Penetration Testing Rights
clause_types.description: Grants the customer the right to conduct security testing of AI systems including traditional penetration testing and AI-specific adversarial testing such as prompt injection, jailbreaking, data extraction, model inversion, and membership inference attacks. Addresses the unique attack surface of AI systems that extends beyond traditional application security.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the scope of permitted testing: network penetration testing, application security testing, API security testing, and AI-specific adversarial testing (prompt injection, jailbreaking, data poisoning simulation, model extraction attempts, adversarial inputs). Specify testing frequency: annual comprehensive test plus continuous automated scanning. Address coordination requirements: advance notice, testing windows, emergency stop procedures. Define the AI-specific test scenarios: red-team testing of model safety guardrails, testing of content filtering effectiveness, attempts to extract training data, testing of multi-tenant isolation. Require the provider to remediate findings within defined SLAs by severity (critical: 24 hours, high: 7 days, medium: 30 days, low: 90 days). Address liability for testing-induced service disruption. Specify reporting format and detail level. Include the provider's obligation to conduct its own AI red-team testing and share results.

variations.customer-conducted: Customer conducts all testing with provider cooperation
variations.provider-conducted: Provider conducts testing and shares results; customer may verify
variations.joint: Joint testing programme with shared red-team exercises

clause_types.name: Responsible AI Principles and Commitments
clause_types.description: Embeds responsible AI principles into the contractual relationship, requiring both parties to adhere to defined ethical standards for AI development and deployment. Addresses fairness, accountability, transparency, safety, privacy, and human dignity. Creates enforceable contractual obligations from what might otherwise be aspirational corporate principles, aligned with the EU AI Act's requirements and international responsible AI frameworks.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the responsible AI principles applicable to the engagement: fairness and non-discrimination, transparency and explainability, safety and robustness, privacy and data protection, accountability, human autonomy and oversight, environmental sustainability. Specify how each principle translates into operational obligations. Require the provider to maintain a responsible AI policy and share it with the customer. Establish a joint responsible AI review process for high-risk use cases. Define prohibited uses: mass surveillance, social scoring, manipulation of vulnerable populations, and other uses prohibited by EU AI Act Art 5. Require impact assessments for high-risk deployments. Address environmental sustainability: energy consumption reporting, carbon footprint of model training and inference. Specify escalation paths for ethical concerns. Include whistleblower protections for responsible AI issues. Define consequences for violation of responsible AI commitments.

variations.principles-based: High-level responsible AI principles with periodic review
variations.standards-based: Alignment with ISO/IEC 42001 and NIST AI RMF with certification requirements
variations.enforceable: Responsible AI obligations as material contractual terms with breach remedies

clause_types.name: Automated Decision-Making Safeguards
clause_types.description: Implements the safeguards required when AI systems make or materially assist decisions that produce legal effects or similarly significantly affect individuals, as required by GDPR Art 22 and the EU AI Act. Establishes the boundaries of automated decision-making, the right to human intervention, the obligation to provide meaningful information, and the right to contest automated decisions.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define which decisions within the service scope constitute 'automated decision-making' under GDPR Art 22. Classify decisions by impact level: fully automated with legal effects, semi-automated with significant effects, automated with human review, and purely informational. For fully automated decisions with legal effects, establish: the legal basis (explicit consent, contractual necessity, or EU/member state law), suitable safeguards per Art 22(3) (right to obtain human intervention, express point of view, contest decision), and specific measures for special category data under Art 22(4). Require the provider to implement technical mechanisms enabling human override. Specify how data subjects are informed of automated processing per Art 13(2)(f). Define the escalation process for contested decisions. Address the interaction between AI system outputs and human decision-makers (is a human merely rubber-stamping AI recommendations). Include logging of all automated decisions for audit purposes.

variations.human-in-the-loop: All AI outputs require human approval before implementation
variations.human-on-the-loop: AI operates autonomously with human monitoring and override capability
variations.tiered: Level of human involvement varies by decision impact and risk classification

clause_types.name: Human Oversight Mechanisms
clause_types.description: Defines the human oversight requirements for AI system operation, specifying the roles, capabilities, and authority of human operators who monitor and control AI system behaviour. Implements the EU AI Act Art 14 requirement that high-risk AI systems be designed to allow effective oversight by natural persons, including the ability to understand system capabilities, monitor operation, and intervene or override system decisions.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the human oversight model: human-in-the-loop (human approves each decision), human-on-the-loop (human monitors and can intervene), or human-in-command (human sets parameters and reviews outcomes). Specify the qualifications and training requirements for human overseers. Define the technical capabilities the AI system must provide to support oversight: real-time dashboards, alert mechanisms, override controls, audit trails, and kill switches. Establish the circumstances requiring human intervention: confidence scores below threshold, novel or edge-case inputs, decisions affecting vulnerable individuals, high-value decisions. Specify response time requirements for human overseers. Address fatigue and automation bias: measures to prevent human overseers from rubber-stamping AI decisions. Include logging of all human oversight actions. Define escalation procedures when human override is exercised. Address liability allocation when human oversight fails or is inadequate.

variations.in-the-loop: Human approves every AI output before it takes effect
variations.on-the-loop: Human monitors AI operation with authority to intervene at any time
variations.in-command: Human sets parameters and reviews outcomes; AI operates within defined boundaries

clause_types.name: AI Model Version Control and Update Management
clause_types.description: Establishes the requirements for managing AI model versions, including the notification and approval process for model updates, the customer's right to pin specific model versions, rollback capabilities, and the testing requirements before new model versions are deployed in production. Addresses the risk that model updates may alter behaviour in ways that affect the customer's use case.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define model versioning nomenclature (major.minor.patch with semantic meaning). Specify notification requirements for each type of update: major version changes (minimum 90 days advance notice), minor updates (30 days), patches (7 days or immediate for security). Establish the customer's right to pin a specific model version for a defined period. Define end-of-support timelines for old versions. Require the provider to publish release notes describing behavioural changes. Establish acceptance testing requirements: customer may test new versions in a staging environment before production deployment. Define rollback rights: customer may revert to previous version within defined period. Address the impact of version changes on SLA benchmarks. Specify whether the customer may run parallel versions during transition. Address the cost implications of version pinning (may require dedicated infrastructure). Include the provider's obligation to maintain compatibility within the same major version.

variations.auto-update: Provider updates models automatically with notification; customer may opt out within window
variations.customer-controlled: Customer explicitly approves all model updates before deployment
variations.staged-rollout: New versions deployed to staging first; promoted to production after customer acceptance

clause_types.name: AI Training Restrictions and Prohibitions
clause_types.description: Imposes specific restrictions on how the AI provider may train, retrain, or fine-tune its AI models using the customer's data, interactions, feedback, and usage patterns. Addresses the commercial concern that the customer's data and usage may improve the provider's general product to the benefit of competitors, and the legal concern that training on customer data may violate data protection laws or contractual confidentiality obligations.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the restricted training activities: initial model training, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), evaluation, and benchmarking. Specify which customer data is subject to training restrictions: raw input data, output data, interaction logs, feedback, usage patterns, and any derivatives. Establish clear prohibitions: the provider shall not use customer data to train, improve, or evaluate any general-purpose model or any model used for other customers. Address indirect training: aggregated or anonymised customer data used for model improvements. Specify exceptions if any: bug fixes, security improvements, performance optimisation. Require technical controls: data segregation, training pipeline access controls, audit logging. Include audit rights to verify compliance with training restrictions. Define contractual remedies for breach: material breach with termination right, indemnification, injunctive relief. Address the provider's sub-processors and their training restrictions.

variations.full-prohibition: No use of customer data for any training or model improvement purpose
variations.anonymised-permitted: Anonymised and aggregated data may be used for general model improvement
variations.opt-in: Customer data used for training only with explicit opt-in consent per use case

clause_types.name: AI Output Indemnification
clause_types.description: Provides indemnification for claims arising from AI-generated outputs, including intellectual property infringement claims (where AI output resembles or reproduces copyrighted material), defamation claims (where AI generates false statements about identifiable individuals), and regulatory enforcement actions resulting from AI outputs. Addresses the provider's liability for outputs that cause third-party harm.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the scope of indemnified claims: IP infringement claims arising from AI outputs, defamation or privacy claims from generated content, regulatory fines or penalties resulting from AI outputs, and third-party commercial losses caused by erroneous AI outputs. Specify the indemnification cap (may be higher than general liability cap given output risk). Establish standard exclusions: customer modifications to outputs, use of outputs outside intended purpose, customer's failure to implement recommended human review. Require the provider to implement content filtering and output safety measures. Include cure rights: modify the service to prevent recurrence, obtain licences for infringing content, provide alternative non-infringing outputs. Address the challenge of output provenance: how to establish that a harmful output came from the provider's system. Specify the notice, cooperation, and defence control procedures. Include an obligation for the provider to maintain IP indemnity insurance.

variations.broad: Provider indemnifies for all claims arising from AI outputs within normal use
variations.ip-only: Indemnification limited to intellectual property infringement claims
variations.tiered: Full indemnity for outputs within specified use cases; limited indemnity for other uses

clause_types.name: AI Hallucination Liability and Mitigation
clause_types.description: Addresses the contractual treatment of AI hallucinations, defined as outputs that are factually incorrect, fabricated, or inconsistent with the training data or ground truth. Establishes the provider's obligations to minimise hallucinations, the acceptable hallucination rate, the liability consequences when hallucinated outputs cause harm, and the mitigation measures that both parties must implement.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define 'Hallucination' in the contract context: outputs that are factually incorrect, internally inconsistent, fabricated, or not grounded in the input data or knowledge base. Establish an acceptable hallucination rate threshold (varies by use case: near-zero for medical/legal, higher tolerance for creative applications). Specify measurement methodology for hallucination rates. Define the provider's mitigation obligations: retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), grounding mechanisms, confidence scoring, citation requirements, and output validation pipelines. Allocate liability: provider liable when hallucination rate exceeds agreed threshold and customer relied on output in accordance with intended use. Address contributory factors: poor input data quality, use outside intended scope, failure to implement recommended human review. Require incident reporting for hallucination-related harms. Include specific remediation obligations: root cause analysis, model retraining, updated guardrails. Address disclaimers and limitations on output reliance.

variations.strict-liability: Provider liable for all hallucination-related harms within intended use, regardless of threshold
variations.threshold-based: Provider liable only when hallucination rate exceeds agreed threshold
variations.shared-responsibility: Liability shared based on whether customer implemented required validation and human review

clause_types.name: AI Content Filtering and Safety Controls
clause_types.description: Requires the AI provider to implement content filtering, safety controls, and output guardrails to prevent the generation of harmful, illegal, or inappropriate content. Addresses the provider's obligations for input filtering (blocking harmful prompts), output filtering (preventing harmful responses), and the configuration of safety controls to balance safety with the customer's legitimate use requirements.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the categories of content to be filtered: illegal content (CSAM, terrorism, incitement), harmful content (self-harm instructions, weapons manufacture), regulated content (medical advice, legal advice, financial advice), and objectionable content (hate speech, harassment). Specify the filtering standards by category: zero-tolerance for illegal content, configurable thresholds for other categories. Address the customer's ability to configure content filtering levels (enterprise customers may need less restrictive filtering for legitimate use cases). Require the provider to document and disclose filtering mechanisms. Address false positives: over-filtering that blocks legitimate use. Establish an appeal/override process for blocked content with audit logging. Specify the provider's update obligations as threats evolve. Address the interaction between content filtering and the customer's acceptable use policy. Require testing of filtering effectiveness. Define liability for content that bypasses filters.

variations.standard: Provider's default safety configuration applied uniformly
variations.configurable: Customer may adjust filtering levels within provider-defined boundaries
variations.custom: Bespoke content filtering configuration for the customer's specific use case

clause_types.name: AI Usage Metrics and Reporting
clause_types.description: Defines the provider's obligations to track, report, and provide transparency on AI system usage metrics, including token consumption, API call volumes, model performance statistics, cost allocation, and user adoption patterns. Ensures the customer has sufficient visibility into how AI services are being consumed and performing to make informed commercial and operational decisions.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the metrics to be tracked and reported: token consumption (input/output), API call volumes (by endpoint, model, and user), latency statistics (p50, p95, p99), error rates, model version distribution, usage by department or use case, cost breakdown per usage category. Define reporting frequency: real-time dashboards for operational metrics, weekly summaries, monthly detailed reports. Require the provider to offer a self-service analytics interface. Specify data retention for historical metrics (minimum 12 months). Address the customer's right to export usage data. Define alerting thresholds for unusual usage patterns (cost spikes, error rate increases). Address budget management: spending alerts, hard caps, automatic throttling. Specify the format and delivery mechanism for reports. Include the customer's right to define custom metrics and reports. Address the accuracy and completeness of usage data.

variations.basic: Monthly usage summary with token counts and cost breakdown
variations.detailed: Real-time dashboards with granular per-user and per-model metrics
variations.enterprise: Custom reporting with budget management, forecasting, and chargeback integration

clause_types.name: AI Model Deprecation and End-of-Life Management
clause_types.description: Establishes the provider's obligations when discontinuing or deprecating an AI model, including minimum notice periods, migration assistance, continued support during the transition, and the availability of replacement models. Addresses the significant operational risk when a model that the customer's workflows depend on is retired, and the regulatory requirement under the EU AI Act for providers of high-risk systems to ensure ongoing support.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define deprecation stages: deprecation announcement, feature freeze, end of active development, end of security updates, end of availability. Specify minimum notice periods for each stage: minimum 12 months from deprecation announcement to end of availability for production models. Require the provider to offer a replacement model with comparable or superior capabilities. Define migration assistance obligations: compatibility analysis, prompt migration guidance, performance benchmarking of replacement model, parallel running period. Address pricing during deprecation: no price increases for deprecated models during notice period. Specify the customer's right to extend the deprecation period for critical use cases (at additional cost if necessary). Address the interaction with model version pinning rights. Require the provider to publish a model lifecycle policy. Include the right to negotiate bespoke end-of-life terms for critical deployments. Address the archival of model outputs and decision logs from the deprecated model.

variations.standard: 12-month notice with replacement model offered and basic migration guidance
variations.extended: 18-month notice with dedicated migration support and parallel running period
variations.enterprise: Custom deprecation timeline negotiated per model with committed migration resources

contract_types.name: AI Services Agreement
contract_types.description: Comprehensive agreement for the provision of AI-powered services, including model development, deployment, hosting, and ongoing operation. Covers the unique aspects of AI service delivery: model performance standards, training data rights, output ownership, bias monitoring, explainability requirements, and responsible AI obligations. The primary agreement type for enterprise AI procurement where the customer engages a provider to deliver AI capabilities as a managed service.

contract_types.name: AI Cloud Services Agreement
contract_types.description: Agreement for the provision of AI capabilities through cloud infrastructure, including GPU compute, model hosting, inference endpoints, and managed AI platform services. Addresses the cloud-specific aspects of AI delivery: multi-tenancy isolation, data residency, infrastructure security, availability, and exit planning. Distinct from a general AI services agreement in its focus on the infrastructure and platform layer rather than the AI outputs themselves.
contract_types.typical_parties: AI Cloud Platform Provider <-> Customer / AI Developer

contract_types.name: AI API Licence Agreement
contract_types.description: Agreement granting access to AI capabilities through an application programming interface, including usage rights, rate limits, acceptable use policies, and output handling terms. The most common form of AI procurement for organisations integrating AI into their own products and services. Addresses API-specific concerns: versioning, deprecation, rate limiting, token pricing, and the distinction between the API service and the underlying model.
contract_types.typical_parties: AI API Provider <-> Developer / Enterprise Customer

contract_types.name: AI Data Sharing Agreement
contract_types.description: Agreement governing the sharing of data between parties for AI and machine learning purposes, including training data provision, data labelling services, benchmark dataset licensing, and collaborative data initiatives. Addresses the specific concerns of data sharing for AI: permitted training uses, data quality warranties, bias in datasets, provenance documentation, and the rights in any AI models trained on the shared data.
contract_types.typical_parties: Data Provider <-> AI Developer / Data Consumer

contract_types.name: AI Model Licence Agreement
contract_types.description: Agreement licensing an AI model (including foundation models, fine-tuned models, and specialised models) for use by the licensee. Covers the model weights, associated documentation, acceptable use restrictions, modification rights, redistribution terms, and the EU AI Act obligations for general-purpose AI models. Distinct from an API licence in that the licensee receives the model itself rather than accessing it via a hosted service.
contract_types.typical_parties: Model Developer / AI Company <-> Licensee / Enterprise Customer

contract_types.name: AI Technology Partnership Agreement
contract_types.description: Strategic collaboration agreement between organisations for the joint development, deployment, or commercialisation of AI technology. Covers joint IP ownership, co-development methodologies, commercialisation rights, revenue sharing, exclusivity arrangements, and the governance of the partnership. Addresses the complex IP allocation challenges when multiple parties contribute data, models, and expertise to create new AI capabilities.
contract_types.typical_parties: AI Technology Partner A <-> AI Technology Partner B

risk_patterns.name: No AI Output Ownership Clause
risk_patterns.trigger: AI services agreement lacks any provision addressing ownership of AI-generated outputs
risk_patterns.description: Without an express output ownership clause, ownership of AI-generated content is legally uncertain in most jurisdictions because AI outputs may not qualify for copyright protection due to the absence of a human author. This creates disputes over who may use, modify, and commercialise the outputs.
risk_patterns.impact: Customer may lack clear rights to use AI-generated deliverables commercially. Potential IP disputes with provider over output exploitation rights. Risk of third-party claims if outputs are commercialised without clear title.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Search for 'output', 'generated content', 'AI deliverables', and 'intellectual property' clauses. If no provision addresses AI-specific output ownership, flag as critical.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add a comprehensive AI output ownership clause defining ownership allocation, licence fallback, and representations of non-infringement. Consider the US Copyright Office position that purely AI-generated works are not copyrightable and build contractual protections accordingly.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: The US Copyright Office has refused registration for purely AI-generated works (Thaler v Perlmutter, 2023), leaving commercial exploitation of AI outputs dependent on contractual rights rather than statutory copyright.

risk_patterns.name: Unrestricted Training Data Usage
risk_patterns.trigger: AI provider's terms permit use of customer data for general model training without restriction or opt-out
risk_patterns.description: Many AI providers include terms permitting them to use customer input data, output data, and usage patterns to train and improve their general models. This effectively means the customer's proprietary data, trade secrets, and personal data contribute to a model used by competitors. Creates GDPR compliance issues where personal data is involved and commercial risk from data leakage through model memorisation.
risk_patterns.impact: Customer's proprietary data and trade secrets used to improve a product available to competitors. GDPR violations if personal data is used for training without adequate legal basis. Risk of data extraction through model memorisation and prompt engineering.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Review 'data usage', 'model improvement', 'service improvement', and 'analytics' clauses. Flag any provision permitting use of customer data for model training, improvement, or development purposes.
risk_patterns.remediation: Negotiate a full training restriction or, at minimum, an opt-out right. Require contractual commitments that customer data will not be used to train general-purpose models. Include audit rights to verify compliance and breach remedies including indemnification.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Samsung banned employee use of ChatGPT after discovering proprietary source code had been submitted as prompts, potentially entering training data. Several class actions have been filed against AI companies for training on copyrighted material.

risk_patterns.name: No Hallucination Liability Framework
risk_patterns.trigger: AI services agreement contains no provisions addressing liability for factually incorrect or fabricated AI outputs
risk_patterns.description: AI systems, particularly large language models, generate outputs that may be factually incorrect, internally inconsistent, or entirely fabricated (hallucinations). Without contractual provisions addressing hallucination liability, the customer bears all risk from relying on incorrect AI outputs, and the provider has no contractual obligation to minimise hallucination rates or implement grounding mechanisms.
risk_patterns.impact: Business decisions made on fabricated AI outputs. Regulatory exposure if hallucinated content is published or relied upon for compliance purposes. Reputational damage from incorrect AI-generated communications.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Search for 'accuracy', 'hallucination', 'correctness', 'reliability', and 'output quality' provisions. Check whether the agreement includes any warranty regarding AI output accuracy or any liability for incorrect outputs.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add hallucination liability clause defining acceptable hallucination rates, measurement methodology, provider mitigation obligations, and liability allocation for hallucination-related harms. Include human oversight requirements for high-risk use cases.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: A New York lawyer was sanctioned after submitting a court brief containing fabricated case citations generated by ChatGPT (Mata v Avianca, 2023). Air Canada was held liable for a chatbot hallucination about bereavement fare policies.

risk_patterns.name: Missing Bias Monitoring Obligations
risk_patterns.trigger: AI system used for decisions affecting individuals without any contractual bias monitoring or fairness requirements
risk_patterns.description: AI systems deployed without bias monitoring may produce discriminatory outcomes across protected characteristics, exposing the deployer to equality and anti-discrimination enforcement actions. The EU AI Act requires providers of high-risk AI systems to implement measures to detect and mitigate bias, and deployers have independent monitoring obligations.
risk_patterns.impact: Discrimination claims from affected individuals. Regulatory enforcement under equality legislation and EU AI Act. Reputational damage from publicised algorithmic bias. Potential class action exposure.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Check whether the AI system is used for decisions affecting individuals (hiring, lending, insurance, access to services). If so, verify the contract includes bias monitoring, fairness metrics, and remediation obligations.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add bias monitoring clause with defined fairness metrics, testing frequency, threshold triggers, and remediation obligations. Ensure compliance with EU AI Act Art 10 data governance requirements and Art 9 risk management system.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Amazon discontinued an AI recruiting tool after discovering it systematically disadvantaged female candidates. Apple Card faced investigation for gender-based credit limit disparities in its AI underwriting system.

risk_patterns.name: Missing Explainability Provisions for Automated Decisions
risk_patterns.trigger: AI system makes automated decisions with legal or significant effects on individuals without explainability capabilities or documentation
risk_patterns.description: GDPR Art 22 requires suitable safeguards for automated decision-making that produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects individuals, including the right to obtain human intervention and an explanation. The EU AI Act Art 13 requires high-risk AI systems to be sufficiently transparent for users to interpret and use outputs. Contracts lacking explainability provisions leave the deployer unable to meet these obligations.
risk_patterns.impact: GDPR enforcement for failure to provide meaningful information about automated decision logic. EU AI Act non-compliance for high-risk systems. Inability to respond to data subject requests for explanation. Potential invalidity of automated decisions.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Determine if the AI system is involved in decisions producing legal or significant effects on individuals. Check for GDPR Art 22 compliance provisions, explainability commitments, and the ability to generate human-readable explanations of individual decisions.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add explainability clause requiring the provider to deliver global and local explanations, maintain technical documentation per EU AI Act Art 11, and support the deployer in meeting GDPR transparency obligations. Include tiered disclosure mechanisms balancing transparency with trade secret protection.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Dutch courts invalidated the SyRI welfare fraud detection system for lack of transparency (NJCM v Netherlands, 2020). Italy's Garante fined a food delivery platform for opaque algorithmic management of riders.

risk_patterns.name: AI Vendor Lock-In Without Exit Rights
risk_patterns.trigger: AI services agreement lacks data portability, model portability, or exit planning provisions
risk_patterns.description: AI vendor lock-in is more severe than traditional IT lock-in because the customer's investment extends beyond data to include fine-tuned models, accumulated prompt engineering, integrated workflows, and institutional knowledge embedded in the AI system's configuration. Without contractual exit provisions, switching AI providers may require months of re-implementation and loss of accumulated model performance.
risk_patterns.impact: Inability to migrate to alternative providers without significant cost and disruption. Loss of fine-tuned models and accumulated learning. Provider may exploit lock-in through price increases or service degradation.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Check for data portability, model portability, exit planning, and transition assistance clauses. Verify that the customer retains ownership of fine-tuned models and can export them in standard formats.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add comprehensive exit provisions: data portability in open formats, model portability (ONNX, SafeTensors), exit planning from contract inception, transition assistance obligations, and pricing protections during the exit period. Require annual exit readiness testing.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: The EU Data Act (2024) introduces portability and switching requirements for cloud services. Organisations that adopted single-vendor AI strategies have reported 40-60% cost increases at renewal due to switching cost leverage.

risk_patterns.name: Missing Human Oversight for High-Risk AI
risk_patterns.trigger: High-risk AI system deployed without defined human oversight mechanisms, override capabilities, or human-in-the-loop requirements
risk_patterns.description: The EU AI Act Art 14 requires high-risk AI systems to be designed to allow effective human oversight. Deployers under Art 26 must assign competent human overseers and ensure they can understand, monitor, and intervene in AI system operation. Without contractual human oversight provisions, the deployer may be unable to meet these obligations and loses the ability to prevent harmful AI decisions.
risk_patterns.impact: EU AI Act non-compliance with fines up to EUR 15 million or 3% of global turnover. Inability to prevent harmful automated decisions. Loss of GDPR Art 22 defence (no human intervention available). Potential criminal liability in jurisdictions with AI governance laws.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Classify the AI system's risk level under EU AI Act Annex III. For high-risk systems, verify the contract includes human oversight mechanisms, override capabilities, operator qualification requirements, and monitoring dashboards.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add human oversight clause specifying the oversight model (in-the-loop, on-the-loop, or in-command), technical capabilities the AI system must provide (dashboards, alerts, override controls, kill switches), operator qualification requirements, and escalation procedures. Align with EU AI Act Art 14 requirements.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: The EU AI Act entered into force in August 2024 with phased application through 2027. Organisations deploying high-risk AI systems without human oversight face substantial administrative fines and potential prohibition of the AI system.

risk_patterns.name: Training Data IP Infringement Exposure
risk_patterns.trigger: AI model trained on data without verified licensing, copyright clearance, or fair use analysis
risk_patterns.description: AI models trained on unlicensed copyrighted material expose both the provider and the deployer to copyright infringement claims. The legal status of using copyrighted material for AI training varies by jurisdiction: the EU provides a text and data mining exception with an opt-out right, while US fair use defences remain uncertain pending litigation. Without contractual protections, the deployer inherits the provider's training data risk.
risk_patterns.impact: Copyright infringement claims from content owners. Injunctive relief preventing use of the AI model. Substantial damages exposure (statutory damages of up to USD 150,000 per work in the US). Requirement to retrain models on licensed data.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Check for training data representations and warranties: provenance documentation, licensing status, copyright compliance. Verify the agreement includes IP indemnification covering training data claims.
risk_patterns.remediation: Require the provider to represent that training data was lawfully sourced and licensed. Include IP indemnification covering claims arising from training data. Require compliance with EU DSM Directive opt-out rights. Maintain training data documentation per EU AI Act Art 53.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: The New York Times sued OpenAI and Microsoft for copyright infringement in training AI models on its articles. Getty Images sued Stability AI for training Stable Diffusion on its copyrighted photographs. Multiple class actions are pending against AI companies for training data practices.

risk_patterns.name: Unplanned Model Deprecation
risk_patterns.trigger: AI provider may deprecate or discontinue models without minimum notice period or migration assistance
risk_patterns.description: AI providers frequently update, replace, or discontinue models as technology advances. Without contractual deprecation protections, the customer may face abrupt model changes that break integrated workflows, alter output quality, or require emergency migration. The fast pace of AI model development means models may have shorter lifecycles than traditional software.
risk_patterns.impact: Disruption to business-critical AI workflows. Degraded output quality during forced migration. Emergency migration costs and resource diversion. Potential regulatory issues if model changes affect compliance posture.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Search for 'deprecation', 'end of life', 'model changes', 'version', and 'discontinuation' provisions. Check minimum notice periods and migration assistance obligations.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add model deprecation clause requiring minimum 12 months notice, a comparable replacement model, migration assistance, parallel running period, and pricing protections during transition. Include model version pinning rights and end-of-support commitments.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: OpenAI deprecated its Codex and earlier GPT models with relatively short notice, forcing enterprises to migrate integrations. Several organisations reported production outages when model changes altered output formats and quality characteristics.

risk_patterns.name: Undefined AI Processing Location
risk_patterns.trigger: AI services agreement does not specify where model training, inference, and data storage occur geographically
risk_patterns.description: AI processing often spans multiple jurisdictions: training data may be processed in one region, model weights stored in another, and inference performed in a third. Without data residency commitments, the customer cannot ensure compliance with GDPR cross-border transfer restrictions, data localisation laws, or sector-specific residency requirements. GPU scarcity may cause providers to process data wherever capacity is available.
risk_patterns.impact: GDPR cross-border transfer violations with fines up to 4% of global turnover. Non-compliance with national data localisation requirements. Loss of customer confidence for sensitive data processing. Potential regulatory prohibition of the AI processing activity.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Check for data residency, processing location, and cross-border transfer provisions. Verify whether the agreement distinguishes between training location, inference location, and data storage location.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add data residency clause specifying permitted locations for all AI processing activities. Distinguish between data storage, model training, and inference locations. Implement GDPR transfer mechanisms for any cross-border flows. Require advance notification of processing location changes.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Following Schrems II, several EU data protection authorities have investigated cloud AI services for inadequate transfer safeguards. The EDPB has issued guidance specifically addressing cross-border AI processing and the applicability of GDPR transfer restrictions to model training.

risk_patterns.name: Missing AI Content Safety Controls
risk_patterns.trigger: Customer-facing AI system deployed without content filtering, safety controls, or output guardrails
risk_patterns.description: AI systems without adequate content filtering may generate harmful, offensive, defamatory, or illegal content that is attributed to the deploying organisation. The EU AI Act requires providers to implement appropriate safety measures, and the Digital Services Act creates obligations for platforms distributing AI-generated content. Deployers face reputational and legal exposure for unfiltered AI outputs.
risk_patterns.impact: Reputational damage from AI-generated harmful content attributed to the organisation. Regulatory enforcement under EU AI Act and Digital Services Act. Defamation or privacy claims from individuals mentioned in AI outputs. Loss of customer trust.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Verify the agreement includes content filtering obligations, safety controls, and output guardrails. Check for acceptable use policies, prohibited content categories, and the customer's ability to configure filtering levels.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add content filtering clause requiring zero-tolerance filtering for illegal content, configurable filtering for other categories, false positive management, and regular testing of filtering effectiveness. Include the provider's obligation to update filters as threats evolve.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Microsoft's Tay chatbot was manipulated to generate racist content within 24 hours of launch. DPD's AI chatbot generated profanity and criticism of the company after prompt injection. These incidents caused significant reputational damage and led to immediate service withdrawal.

risk_patterns.name: Multi-Tenant AI Data Leakage Risk
risk_patterns.trigger: AI service operates in multi-tenant architecture without defined isolation controls or tenant separation guarantees
risk_patterns.description: Multi-tenant AI systems process data from multiple customers on shared infrastructure, creating the risk that one tenant's data leaks to another through model context pollution, shared caching, logging aggregation, or inadequate access controls. AI-specific leakage vectors include cross-tenant prompt injection, model context carryover between requests, and embedding similarity attacks that reveal information about other tenants' data.
risk_patterns.impact: Confidential data and trade secrets exposed to competing organisations. GDPR breach notification obligations. Regulatory enforcement for inadequate technical and organisational measures. Loss of customer confidence and contract terminations.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Determine whether the AI service is multi-tenant. Check for tenant isolation commitments, data segregation controls, and the provider's technical measures to prevent cross-tenant data leakage.
risk_patterns.remediation: Add multi-tenancy isolation clause specifying technical controls: tenant-specific encryption, request isolation, cache segregation, logging separation, and access controls. Require regular penetration testing of isolation boundaries. Consider dedicated instances for sensitive workloads. Include breach notification and remediation obligations specific to isolation failures.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: Researchers have demonstrated prompt injection attacks that extract other users' data from shared AI systems. Context window leakage between requests has been documented in several multi-tenant LLM deployments. These vulnerabilities are actively exploited in adversarial settings.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: AI provider's terms permit use of customer data for general model training with no opt-out
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Allowing unrestricted training on customer data means the customer's proprietary information, trade secrets, and potentially personal data contribute to a model available to competitors. This is commercially unreasonable for enterprise deployments and creates GDPR compliance risk where personal data is involved.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise AI contracts: full training restriction is becoming standard. Mid-market: opt-out right is minimum. Consumer: opt-out typically available in settings.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Require a contractual commitment that customer data will not be used for general model training, fine-tuning, or evaluation. At minimum, negotiate an opt-out right with confirmation. Include audit rights to verify compliance.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Contract clearly assigns all AI-generated output rights to the customer upon creation and payment
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Clear customer ownership of AI outputs provides the certainty needed for commercial exploitation, even where statutory copyright may not subsist. The contractual assignment creates enforceable rights regardless of the jurisdictional uncertainty about AI copyright.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise AI services: customer ownership of outputs is standard. API services: varies, but customer ownership of outputs from paid tiers is increasingly common.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Accept. Verify the clause covers all output types (text, code, images, data), includes a licence fallback if copyright does not subsist, and contains representations of non-infringement.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: AI services agreement contains no provision addressing ownership of AI-generated outputs
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Without an express output ownership clause, the customer has no clear legal basis to use, modify, or commercialise AI-generated outputs. Given that AI outputs may not qualify for copyright in many jurisdictions, the absence of contractual allocation leaves both parties in legal uncertainty.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: All enterprise AI contracts should address AI output ownership explicitly. Silence on this point is below market standard and indicates the agreement was not drafted for AI services.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Require insertion of an AI output ownership clause. Propose customer ownership with licence fallback, representations of non-infringement, and clear definition of what constitutes an AI output versus human-authored work.
