negotiation_intelligence.condition: Provider disclaims all responsibility for AI output accuracy with no performance standard or hallucination threshold
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: A blanket accuracy disclaimer removes the provider's incentive to maintain output quality and leaves the customer bearing all risk from AI errors. While some uncertainty in AI outputs is inherent, enterprise agreements should include minimum performance standards and a shared responsibility framework for output quality.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise: accuracy thresholds with service credits for degradation. Mid-market: accuracy benchmarks with remediation obligations. Consumer: disclaimer with best-efforts quality commitment.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Counter with defined accuracy metrics (precision, recall, F1 score) measured against an agreed benchmark dataset. Include a hallucination rate threshold with remediation obligations. Accept that the provider is not a guarantor of output correctness, but require reasonable performance standards.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Provider reserves right to update AI models without notice and customer cannot pin to a specific version
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Uncontrolled model updates can alter output behaviour, break integrated workflows, and change the risk profile of the AI service. While providers need the flexibility to improve models and address safety issues, enterprise customers require stability and predictability in their AI integrations.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise: model version pinning with 90-day notice for major changes. Mid-market: 30-day notice with opt-out for major versions. Standard: provider-controlled updates with changelog.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Negotiate model version pinning rights with minimum 90-day advance notice for major version changes. Require a staging environment for pre-deployment testing. Include rollback rights for 30 days after any model update. Accept automatic security patches with notification.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Provider provides IP indemnification for AI-generated outputs within defined use cases
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: IP indemnification for AI outputs protects the customer against claims that the AI-generated content infringes third-party intellectual property. Given the known risk that AI models may reproduce or closely replicate copyrighted training data, this indemnification is a critical commercial protection.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Leading AI providers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) now offer AI output IP indemnification for enterprise tiers. This is becoming the market standard for paid AI services.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Accept. Verify the indemnification covers copyright, patent, and trademark claims. Check for carve-outs (customer modifications, use outside intended purpose, combination with third-party content). Ensure the indemnity is not capped at a nominal amount.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: AI services agreement contains no data portability, model portability, or exit planning provisions
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Without exit provisions, the customer is locked into the provider with no contractual mechanism for an orderly transition. AI lock-in is more severe than traditional IT because the customer's investment includes fine-tuned models, prompt engineering, accumulated learning, and integrated workflows that cannot easily be reproduced with a new provider.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise: comprehensive exit provisions with 12-month transition are standard. Mid-market: data portability and 6-month exit assistance. The EU Data Act will mandate switching provisions for cloud services from September 2025.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Require comprehensive exit provisions: data export in open formats within 30 days, model export (if fine-tuned) in ONNX or equivalent, exit plan documented at contract inception, 12-month transition period with continued service at current pricing, and migration assistance at cost.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Provider claims ownership of fine-tuned models created using the customer's data and at the customer's expense
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: When the customer provides the training data and pays for the fine-tuning, the resulting model represents the customer's investment and competitive advantage. Provider ownership of fine-tuned models creates lock-in and allows the provider to exploit the customer's data investment for other customers.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Enterprise: customer owns or co-owns fine-tuned models created with customer data. The provider retains the base model. Mid-market: customer has perpetual licence to fine-tuned model. Provider ownership of customer-funded fine-tuning is below market standard.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Negotiate customer ownership of fine-tuned model weights and configuration, with a licence back to the provider for the base model components only. At minimum, require a perpetual, irrevocable licence to the fine-tuned model with portability rights in ONNX or equivalent format.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Contract includes defined human oversight model with override capabilities, operator qualifications, and escalation procedures aligned with EU AI Act Art 14
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Comprehensive human oversight provisions demonstrate EU AI Act readiness, protect against harmful automated decisions, and provide a defence to GDPR Art 22 claims. They also create operational clarity about the roles and responsibilities of human operators monitoring the AI system.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: For high-risk AI systems, human oversight provisions aligned with EU AI Act Art 14 are becoming standard in enterprise agreements. For lower-risk systems, lighter-touch oversight is acceptable.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Accept. Verify the oversight model matches the AI system's risk classification. Ensure the contract specifies technical capabilities (dashboards, alerts, kill switches), operator qualification requirements, and response time SLAs for human intervention.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: AI provider seeks unlimited liability exclusion for all AI output-related claims
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: An unlimited liability exclusion for AI output claims leaves the customer bearing all financial risk from AI errors, hallucinations, biased outputs, and IP infringement in AI-generated content. This is commercially unreasonable when the provider controls the model quality, training data, and safety measures.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: AI output claims should be subject to a super-cap above the general liability cap, typically 2-5x annual fees. Full exclusion of liability for AI outputs is below market standard for enterprise agreements.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Reject the unlimited exclusion. Propose a tiered liability framework: provider liable for output quality within defined performance standards, shared liability for outputs within intended use, and customer assumes risk for use outside intended purpose. Apply a super-cap for AI-specific claims at 2-3x annual fees.

clause_interactions.description: The general AI liability allocation clause may cap or limit the effective value of AI output indemnification, rendering the indemnity insufficient to cover real-world IP infringement or hallucination-related claims.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify whether AI output indemnification is carved out from the general liability cap or subject to a separate super-cap. Ensure the output indemnity cap reflects the realistic exposure from IP infringement and defamation claims arising from AI outputs.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Output indemnification becomes meaningless if capped at the general liability level, which may be far below the cost of defending and settling IP infringement claims.

clause_interactions.description: Training restrictions prohibiting the use of customer data for model training may conflict with synthetic data clauses that permit generation of synthetic datasets derived from customer data, if the synthetic data is subsequently used for model training purposes.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that training restrictions explicitly address whether synthetic data derived from customer data is subject to the same restrictions. Define whether generating synthetic data from customer data constitutes 'use for training purposes' under the training restriction clause.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Provider circumvents training restrictions by generating synthetic data from customer data and using the synthetic data for model training, achieving the restricted outcome through an indirect method.

clause_interactions.description: Effective human oversight of AI systems requires sufficient explainability for the human overseer to understand the AI system's reasoning and make informed decisions about whether to approve, modify, or override its outputs.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the explainability provisions provide the level of detail needed for the defined human oversight model. A human-in-the-loop model requires local explanations for each decision; a human-on-the-loop model requires at minimum global explanations and anomaly alerting.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Human oversight becomes a rubber-stamping exercise if the overseer cannot understand why the AI system produced a particular output, defeating the purpose of the oversight requirement and creating regulatory non-compliance.

clause_interactions.description: Model version control provides the operational mechanism through which model deprecation is managed, including version pinning during transition, parallel running of old and new versions, and controlled migration from deprecated to replacement models.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the version pinning rights in the model versioning clause extend through the deprecation notice period. Ensure the deprecation clause references the versioning mechanism for managing the transition.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Customer loses access to the deprecated model version before migration is complete, or the deprecation process does not leverage version pinning to ensure service continuity during transition.

clause_interactions.description: Data and model portability rights are only exercisable within a structured exit framework that provides the timeline, cooperation, and continued service access needed to execute the migration.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the exit planning clause provides sufficient transition time (6-12 months) for the customer to exercise all portability rights. Ensure the provider's cooperation obligations during exit include assisting with data and model export, not merely making export tools available.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Portability rights exist on paper but cannot be exercised in practice if the exit process is too rushed, the provider is uncooperative, or the service is terminated before migration is complete.

ip_provisions.name: AI-Generated Output Ownership
ip_provisions.description: Allocates ownership of content, code, analyses, and other works generated by AI systems. Addresses the fundamental legal challenge that AI-generated works may not qualify for copyright protection in jurisdictions requiring human authorship, necessitating contractual allocation of rights regardless of copyright status.
drafting_checklist: Define what constitutes AI-generated output versus human-authored work
Specify ownership allocation upon creation and payment
Include licence fallback if copyright does not subsist in AI outputs
Address the distinction between AI-assisted and AI-generated content
Require representations regarding originality and non-infringement
Specify whether customer may claim authorship of AI outputs
Address the treatment of AI outputs that incorporate training data
Include provisions for mixed human-AI works (human editing of AI drafts)
Specify territorial scope of ownership assignment
Address moral rights where applicable to AI-assisted works

risk_considerations.copyright_uncertainty: AI-generated works may not qualify for copyright protection in jurisdictions requiring human authorship, including the US (Thaler v Perlmutter) and most EU member states under the Berne Convention. Contractual assignment provides rights only to the extent they exist.
risk_considerations.joint_authorship: Where a human substantially modifies AI output, the resulting work may qualify as a joint work, creating complex co-ownership questions between the human author and the party controlling the AI system.
risk_considerations.training_data_contamination: AI outputs may incorporate or closely replicate copyrighted material from training data, exposing the customer to IP infringement claims when exploiting outputs commercially.
risk_considerations.moral_rights: In civil law jurisdictions, moral rights attach to works with a human author. The uncertain authorship status of AI outputs creates complexity in moral rights compliance.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Most EU member states require human authorship for copyright. The CJEU has interpreted 'intellectual creation' as requiring human creative choices (Infopaq, Painer). AI outputs without substantial human intervention likely receive no copyright protection.
jurisdiction_flags.US: The US Copyright Office requires human authorship and has refused registration for purely AI-generated works. Works with sufficient human creative control over AI may be registrable per the February 2023 guidance.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: The UK uniquely provides copyright protection for computer-generated works under CDPA 1988 s.9(3), with authorship vesting in the person who made the arrangements necessary for the creation of the work.

ip_provisions.name: Training Data Licence for AI Model Development
ip_provisions.description: Grants the AI developer a licence to use specified datasets for the purpose of training, validating, and evaluating AI models. Addresses the scope of permitted training uses, restrictions on model improvement for third parties, data retention after training, and compliance with copyright text and data mining exceptions.
drafting_checklist: Define the licensed data precisely by category and scope
Specify permitted training uses: initial training, fine-tuning, RLHF, evaluation
Restrict whether data may be used for general model improvement or customer-specific only
Address data retention obligations after model training completion
Include compliance with copyright TDM exceptions and opt-out rights
Require data provenance documentation and audit trail
Specify restrictions on combining licensed data with third-party datasets
Address the risk of data memorisation and regurgitation in model outputs
Include audit rights over data usage during and after training
Specify termination effects on previously trained models

risk_considerations.copyright_compliance: Training data may include copyrighted works. The EU DSM Directive Art 3-4 provides text and data mining exceptions but rights holders can opt out under Art 4. US fair use defence for training data remains uncertain pending NYT v OpenAI and similar litigation.
risk_considerations.data_memorisation: Models may memorise and reproduce training data verbatim, creating copyright infringement risk and potential data breach if personal data is memorised and later output.
risk_considerations.scope_creep: Broadly drafted training licences may permit the provider to use customer data to improve models for competitors, undermining the customer's competitive advantage.
risk_considerations.post_termination: Models trained on licensed data retain the learning even after the licence expires. Address whether previously trained models must be retrained or whether the licence survives for models trained during the licence term.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: EU DSM Directive Art 3 provides a mandatory TDM exception for research organisations. Art 4 provides a broader commercial TDM exception subject to rights holder opt-out. Compliance requires respecting machine-readable opt-out mechanisms (robots.txt, metadata).
jurisdiction_flags.US: Fair use defence for AI training is actively litigated. Thomson Reuters v ROSS Intelligence found no fair use. NYT v OpenAI is pending. The outcome will significantly affect training data licensing requirements.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: The UK proposed but withdrew a broad TDM exception in 2023. Current UK law provides a limited research-only TDM exception under CDPA 1988 s.29A. Commercial AI training requires licensing.

ip_provisions.name: AI Model Weights and Fine-Tuned Model Ownership
ip_provisions.description: Addresses the ownership of AI model weights, including base models, fine-tuned models, and derivative models created during the engagement. Distinguishes between the provider's pre-existing base model IP and the incremental value created through customer-funded fine-tuning, establishing clear ownership boundaries for each layer of the model stack.
drafting_checklist: Distinguish between base model weights (provider IP) and fine-tuning increments (potentially customer IP)
Define what constitutes a 'fine-tuned model' versus a 'derivative model'
Specify ownership of LoRA adapters, embeddings, and other fine-tuning artifacts
Address the portability of fine-tuned models in standard formats (ONNX, SafeTensors)
Include licence back provisions for the provider's base model components
Specify whether the customer may deploy the fine-tuned model on alternative infrastructure
Address the ownership of hyperparameters, training configurations, and evaluation results
Include escrow provisions for model weights
Specify restrictions on reverse-engineering the base model from the fine-tuned model
Address the survival of model ownership rights after contract termination

risk_considerations.inseparability: Fine-tuned model weights are mathematically inseparable from the base model weights. It may be impossible to extract only the customer's contribution, creating practical ownership challenges.
risk_considerations.base_model_dependency: Customer ownership of a fine-tuned model is meaningless without a licence to the underlying base model architecture and inference code needed to run it.
risk_considerations.competitive_use: If the provider retains rights to customer-funded fine-tuning, it may use those improvements to benefit competing customers.
risk_considerations.export_restrictions: Some AI models are subject to export control restrictions that may limit the customer's ability to deploy model weights in certain jurisdictions.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Model weights are likely protected as databases or computer programs under EU IP law. The EU AI Act requires GPAI model providers to maintain documentation of training and testing processes, which may include model weight provenance.
jurisdiction_flags.US: Model weights may be protected as trade secrets and potentially as literary works (copyright in the trained parameters as a compilation). The legal classification remains unsettled.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Model weights likely protected as databases under the Database Right or as computer programs under copyright. UK government has signalled intention to regulate foundation model IP.

ip_provisions.name: Prompt Engineering IP Protection
ip_provisions.description: Protects the intellectual property in prompt engineering assets including system prompts, prompt templates, prompt chains, few-shot examples, and prompt optimisation methodologies. Addresses the challenge that prompts may not qualify for traditional IP protection due to their functional nature or brevity, requiring contractual and confidentiality-based protections.
drafting_checklist: Define protected prompt assets: system prompts, templates, chains, few-shot examples, meta-prompts
Specify ownership: customer prompts vs provider base prompt library
Include confidentiality obligations specifically covering prompt assets
Prohibit reverse-engineering or extraction of system prompts
Address the status of prompt optimisation insights derived from customer usage
Specify whether prompts are included in technology escrow materials
Include non-compete restrictions on prompt methodologies if applicable
Address versioning and documentation requirements for prompt libraries
Specify the treatment of prompts upon contract termination
Include restrictions on the provider sharing customer prompt patterns with other customers

risk_considerations.copyright_limitations: Individual prompts may be too short or too functional to qualify for copyright protection. Prompt libraries as compilations may receive thin copyright protection.
risk_considerations.trade_secret_vulnerability: Prompts are inherently disclosed to the AI provider during use, creating challenges for maintaining trade secret status. System prompts may be extractable through prompt injection techniques.
risk_considerations.competitive_intelligence: Prompt patterns reveal the customer's use cases, strategies, and methodologies. A provider serving competitors may inadvertently transfer competitive intelligence through prompt optimisation insights.
risk_considerations.rapid_obsolescence: Prompt engineering techniques evolve rapidly with each model generation. IP protections must balance current value with the likelihood of obsolescence.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Prompts may qualify for copyright as literary works if they demonstrate sufficient originality (author's own intellectual creation). Databases of prompts may be protected under the Database Directive. Trade secret protection under the EU Trade Secrets Directive requires reasonable secrecy measures.
jurisdiction_flags.US: Short prompts likely uncopyrightable under the merger doctrine (limited ways to express the idea). Prompt compilations may receive thin compilation copyright. Trade secret protection requires demonstrable secrecy measures and independent economic value.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Copyright threshold is lower than EU (skill, labour, and judgment). Prompt compilations more likely protectable. Confidential information protection available for commercially valuable prompts even without copyright.

ip_provisions.name: Training Data Provenance and Chain of Rights
ip_provisions.description: Requires the AI provider to document and warrant the provenance of all data used to train AI models, including the original source, licensing status, copyright clearance, consent basis for personal data, and any restrictions flowing from upstream data licences. Essential for managing the IP infringement risk inherent in AI models trained on large datasets from diverse sources.
drafting_checklist: Require a training data inventory with source and licensing information
Include representations that all training data was lawfully obtained and licensed
Specify compliance with EU DSM Directive opt-out mechanisms
Require documentation of consent basis for any personal data in training sets
Address open-source and Creative Commons licence compliance in training data
Include indemnification for IP claims arising from training data
Specify audit rights over training data provenance records
Address the treatment of web-scraped data and robots.txt compliance
Require ongoing monitoring of training data licensing changes and takedown requests
Include provisions for retraining if training data rights are revoked

risk_considerations.unknown_provenance: Large-scale training datasets often contain works of unknown provenance. The provider may be unable to verify the licensing status of every item in a dataset containing billions of data points.
risk_considerations.chain_of_rights: Training data may pass through multiple aggregators and processors. Verifying the complete chain of rights from original creator to training use is often impractical at scale.
risk_considerations.evolving_legal_landscape: Data that was lawfully used for training under one legal interpretation may become infringing as case law develops. The outcome of pending litigation may retroactively affect training data legality.
risk_considerations.opt_out_compliance: Rights holders may exercise opt-out rights after training has occurred. The provider must address whether already-trained models must be retrained when opt-outs are received.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: EU AI Act Art 53(1)(c) requires GPAI model providers to put in place a policy to comply with EU copyright law, including the TDM opt-out under DSM Directive Art 4. Art 53(1)(d) requires a sufficiently detailed summary of training data.
jurisdiction_flags.US: No comprehensive training data transparency requirement. Pending legislation (AI SHIELD Act, AI Disclosure Act) may introduce requirements. Current obligations arise primarily from copyright law and contractual commitments.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: No specific training data provenance requirement. The UK ICO has issued guidance on the use of personal data for AI training, requiring documented lawful basis and transparency.

ip_provisions.name: AI Output IP Indemnification
ip_provisions.description: Provider indemnifies the customer against intellectual property infringement claims arising from AI-generated outputs, covering copyright, patent, trademark, and trade secret claims by third parties. Addresses the specific risk that AI outputs may reproduce or derive from copyrighted training data, generating infringement liability for the customer who uses the outputs commercially.
drafting_checklist: Define the scope of indemnified IP claims: copyright, patent, trademark, trade secret
Specify that indemnification covers claims arising from normal use of AI outputs
Include standard exclusions: customer modifications, use outside specifications, combination claims
Define cure rights: modify service, obtain licence, provide non-infringing alternative, refund
Specify the indemnification cap (consider super-cap above general liability)
Address the challenge of proving which AI output caused the infringement
Include the provider's obligation to maintain copyright compliance measures
Specify notice, cooperation, and defence control procedures
Address the provider's obligation to maintain IP indemnity insurance
Include the customer's mitigation obligations upon notice of a claim

risk_considerations.training_data_reproduction: AI models may reproduce copyrighted training data in their outputs, exposing the customer to infringement claims. The risk is higher for models trained on large datasets without rigorous deduplication.
risk_considerations.proof_challenges: Establishing that an AI output infringes a specific copyrighted work requires demonstrating substantial similarity, which may be difficult when the output is a probabilistic generation rather than a direct copy.
risk_considerations.emerging_liability: The legal framework for AI output liability is developing rapidly. Indemnification terms agreed today may be inadequate as liability standards evolve through litigation and regulation.
risk_considerations.coverage_gaps: Standard professional indemnity and cyber insurance policies may not cover AI output IP claims. The provider should be required to maintain specific coverage.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: The EU Product Liability Directive (2024/2853) extends product liability to AI systems. Combined with the proposed AI Liability Directive, this creates a framework where providers may face strict liability for defective AI outputs.
jurisdiction_flags.US: Several major AI providers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Adobe) now offer AI output IP indemnification for enterprise customers, establishing market expectations. Indemnification scope varies significantly between providers.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: UK copyright in computer-generated works under CDPA s.9(3) creates unique dynamics: AI outputs may be copyrighted (unlike in the US), but the scope of protection and the risk of infringing third-party works remain uncertain.

ip_provisions.name: Synthetic Data IP and Privacy Rights
ip_provisions.description: Governs the intellectual property and data protection treatment of synthetic data generated by AI systems from real-world source data. Addresses the ownership of synthetic datasets, the extent to which synthetic data inherits restrictions from source data, privacy preservation requirements, and the rights to use synthetic data for downstream AI training and commercial purposes.
drafting_checklist: Define synthetic data and distinguish from anonymised, pseudonymised, and aggregated data
Specify ownership of synthetic datasets generated during the engagement
Address whether synthetic data derived from personal data constitutes personal data
Require privacy impact assessment before synthetic data generation from personal data
Specify differential privacy or other formal privacy guarantees
Define permitted uses: internal training, sharing with third parties, commercial sale
Address restrictions flowing from source data licences into synthetic data
Include re-identification risk assessment and ongoing monitoring obligations
Specify quality metrics: statistical fidelity, utility preservation, privacy measures
Address the treatment of synthetic data upon contract termination

risk_considerations.re_identification_risk: Synthetic data generated without adequate privacy guarantees may allow re-identification of individuals in the source dataset, particularly for outliers or small populations. Regulators may treat inadequately anonymised synthetic data as personal data.
risk_considerations.source_data_restrictions: Synthetic data may inherit contractual restrictions from the source data licence, particularly if the synthetic data is derived from or substantially similar to licensed datasets.
risk_considerations.quality_vs_privacy_tradeoff: Higher statistical fidelity in synthetic data typically means lower privacy protection. Contractual quality metrics must account for this tension.
risk_considerations.regulatory_uncertainty: The regulatory status of synthetic data under GDPR and other data protection laws remains uncertain and varies by the generation methodology and the privacy guarantees applied.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: The EDPB has not issued definitive guidance on whether synthetic data constitutes personal data under GDPR. GDPR Recital 26 states that anonymised data falls outside GDPR scope, but the threshold for adequate anonymisation of AI-generated synthetic data is unclear.
jurisdiction_flags.US: No federal standard for synthetic data classification. The FTC has taken enforcement action where 'anonymised' data was re-identifiable. State privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA) may apply if synthetic data is derived from personal information.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: The UK ICO has published guidance on anonymisation acknowledging synthetic data as a privacy-enhancing technology but noting that re-identification risk assessment is required.

ip_provisions.name: Foundation Model Downstream Rights and Restrictions
ip_provisions.description: Defines the rights and restrictions that flow from the foundation model licence to downstream products, services, and derivative models built on top of the foundation model. Addresses the cascading licence obligations, acceptable use restrictions, and compliance requirements that the customer must flow down to its own customers when building products on licensed foundation models.
drafting_checklist: Identify all upstream foundation model licence restrictions that flow downstream
Specify which restrictions apply to the customer's end products versus internal use
Address acceptable use policy flow-down requirements to end users
Define the customer's obligation to enforce upstream restrictions in its own agreements
Specify attribution and notice requirements for downstream products
Address the treatment of open-source foundation model licences (Llama, Mistral, etc.)
Include compliance representations for downstream use
Specify reporting obligations for downstream usage if required by upstream licence
Address the impact of foundation model licence changes on existing downstream products
Include termination cascade provisions: effect of upstream licence termination on downstream rights

risk_considerations.licence_cascade: Foundation model licence restrictions cascade to all downstream products and services. A restrictive upstream licence may limit the customer's ability to commercialise products built on the model.
risk_considerations.acceptable_use_enforcement: The customer may be obligated to enforce the foundation model provider's acceptable use policy against its own end users, creating operational complexity and potential liability for end user violations.
risk_considerations.licence_changes: Foundation model providers may update their licence terms. If the customer has built products on a foundation model, licence changes may affect the legality of existing products.
risk_considerations.open_source_complexity: Open-weight models (Llama, Mistral) come with use restrictions that are not traditional open-source licences. These hybrid licences create complex compliance obligations for downstream users.

jurisdiction_flags.EU: The EU AI Act creates obligations for providers of GPAI models (Art 51-56) and for deployers of AI systems (Art 26). When building downstream products on foundation models, the customer may become both a downstream provider and a deployer with separate compliance obligations.
jurisdiction_flags.US: US contract law generally permits flow-down of licence restrictions. Export control regulations (EAR) may apply to AI models and restrict downstream distribution to certain countries or end users.
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Post-Brexit, the UK is developing its own AI regulatory framework. Downstream products using foundation models must comply with both the UK framework and any applicable EU requirements if offered in the EU market.
